query: Down syndrome and periodontitis involving molars

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several genetic disorders, including Down syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (types IV and VIII), and Crohn disease, have periodontal manifestations, highlighting the complex interplay between systemic conditions and oral health. [24] [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Gingiva -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Non-modifiable risk factors for gingivitis and associated periodontal disease include age and genetic disorders. The literature shows that the inflammatory response to plaque deposition in older individuals contains a significantly higher number of inflammatory cells than in younger individuals. Additionally, aging increases the risk of inadequate oral hygiene practices, further contributing to the increased risk of gingivitis and periodontal disease in the older population. Finally, genetic disorders such as Down syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and Crohn disease have proven to increase the risk of gingival disease. [16] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Abscess -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: As this infection within the pulp cavity grows within the limited space of the tooth, it compresses the inner dentine walls, causing severe pain. This infection then tracks down through the root canal and inferiorly into the mandible or superiorly into the maxilla, depending on the location of the infected tooth. Another cause that predisposes individuals to a dental abscess is a partially erupted tooth, most commonly a wisdom tooth, where bacteria get trapped between the crown and soft tissues, causing inflammation. Other causes include genetic causes such as imperfect amelogenesis, which predisposes individuals to weakened enamel and makes them more susceptible to wear. Mechanical causes tooth grinding to break down tooth enamel. Medical conditions like Sjogren syndrome cause dry mouth, accelerating oropharyngeal microbial growth. Chemical irritants such as smoke from methamphetamine, immunosuppression arising from chemotherapy, or chronic immunosuppressive medical conditions such as HIV/AIDs can predispose individuals to dental caries. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Taurodontism: It is also referred to as bull's teeth and is *characterized by teeth with abnormally large pulp chambers with furcation displaced apically, resulting in larger than normal apical-occlusal height. Molars are the most commonly affected teeth. It frequently correlates with syndromes such as Klinefelter's syndrome, Mohr syndrome, orofacial digital syndrome, and Down syndrome. Peg laterals: It is commonly seen in the maxillary anterior region affecting the maxillary lateral incisor and is characterized by a tooth that is conical in shape with a taper in the incisal region.

retrieved_docs.title: Cri Du Chat Syndrome -- History and Physical -- (c) Orofacial Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: High palate Mandibular microretrognathia Hypoplasia of the enamel Chronic periodontitis

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosing cracked teeth is notoriously challenging because of their variable presentation. Patients may describe symptoms that overlap with various diagnoses, emphasizing the importance of obtaining an accurate history and conducting diagnostic tests. The most frequently reported symptom is a sudden sharp pain upon biting down on the affected tooth. [21] Additionally, some patients experience a fleeting sharp pain upon the release of bite pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Angular Chelitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The following are alterations in mouth structure leading to changes in lip approximation and increased salivary pooling and maceration at the labial commissures: The normal loss of skin turgor due to aging, smoking, or rapid weight loss Loss of vertical dimension of the face due to severe tooth wear, edentulous states, and mal-fitted dentures increasing overhang of the upper lip onto the lower one (overclosure) Retrognathic malocclusion Deepened furrowing of the skin dependent on the commissures (marionette lines) Conditions associated with enlarged lips such as oro-facial granulomatosis (OFG). Up to 20% of OFG patients suffer from AC, but Candida is usually not isolated from lesions Down syndrome: 25% of patients have AC due to macroglossia leading to tongue protrusion and drooling [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Natal and Neonatal Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Certain syndromes have been reported to be associated with the occurrence of natal and neonatal teeth. These syndromes include chondroectodermal dysplasia, Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome, Pierre Robin syndrome, neonatal progeria, cleft lip or palate, ectodermal dysplasia, craniofacial dysostosis, and Down syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Taurodontism has been commonly attributed to the incomplete invagination of the epithelial root sheath during tooth development, leading to misplaced dentin deposition and an abnormally large pulp chamber (see Image . Dental Taurodontism). [9] [10] This anomaly has been associated with several syndromes, including amelogenesis imperfecta, Klinefelter syndrome, and Down syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Cockayne Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Dental Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: The deciduous teeth may appear late and exhibit characteristics such as being small, irregular, and abnormally spaced. In certain instances, permanent teeth may not develop; when they do, they remain small or poorly implanted. Children affected by this condition are more prone to cavities than the average population.

retrieved_docs.title: Char Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Typical facial features include a depressed nasal bridge and broad flat nasal tip, widely spaced eyes, down-slanted palpebral fissures, mild ptosis, a short philtrum with prominent philtrum ridges with an upward pointing vermilion border resulting in a triangular mouth and thickened (patulous) everted lips. [3] The most commonly identified cardiac anomaly is PDA. [1] Less common findings include other types of congenital heart defects, other hand and foot anomalies, hypodontia, hearing loss, myopia and/or strabismus, polythelia, parasomnia, craniosynostosis (involving either the metopic or sagittal suture), and short stature. [1] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Marcus Gunn Jaw-Winking Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Levator function, which is the distance the upper lid travels from downgaze to upgaze while the frontalis muscle is held firmly at the brow region. Levator function can be graded as normal (at least 15 mm), good (12-14 mm), fair (5-11 mm), or poor (less than or equal to 4 mm).

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Nondisjunction -- Clinical Significance -- Autosomal Trisomies
retrieved_docs.content: Patau syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 13 Clinical Features: Rocker-bottom feet, microphthalmia (abnormally small eyes), microcephaly (abnormally small head), polydactyly, holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and palate, congenital heart disease, and severe intellectual disability. Life expectancy is seldom longer than one year. Edwards syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 18 Clinical Features: Rocker-bottom feet, low set ears, micrognathia (abnormally small jaw), clenched hands with overlapping fingers, congenital heart disease, and severe intellectual disability. Life expectancy is normally less than one year. Down syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 21 The most common viable aneuploidy. Clinical Features: Single palmar crease, flat facies, prominent epicanthal folds, duodenal atresia, congenital heart disease, Hirschsprung disease, intellectual disability. Notably increased risk to develop Alzheimer's disease or leukemia. Life expectancy is about 60 years.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a medical condition that is associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is linked to several findings, including النفخة الانقباضية, حفظ الصحة الروتيني, عرج, and لغط الشريان السباتي. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم, reflecting a more generalized form of the disease. These associations are important for recognizing the clinical presentation of التصلب العصيدي and guiding appropriate evaluation and management.
